data.title: What Sanofi Is Getting Through The Acquisition Of Dynavax
data.description:
Sanofi to acquire Dynavax

Sanofi (SNY) announced the acquisition of Dynavax (DVAX) for $15.50 per share, or $2.2 billion. This could be the last in a series of biotech M&A transactions this year, and it looks like a decent but largely inconsequential acquisition in the near- and medium-term for Sanofi, with potential long-term upside from Dynavax’s early-stage projects.

The deal announcement is also a bit opportunistic, as I believe Sanofi has used it to deflect attention from the complete response letter it received today for tolebrutinib for the treatment of secondary progressive multiple sclerosis, but I also do not see this FDA rejection as significantly impactful on Sanofi’s overall business, as this is a very small indication for tolebrutinib.

On the other hand, I would be at least somewhat disappointed if I were a Dynavax shareholder. The company is selling itself for approximately 7 times this year’s Heplisav B revenue and this implies little to no value assigned to the other pipeline projects.

Heplisav-B alone is worth the price tag

Heplisav-B is a hepatitis B vaccine and Dynavax guided for $315-325 million in U.S. net sales in 2025, a 17-21% increase over 2024.

Q3 net sales have already reached $90 million which represents approximately 46% of the U.S. market.

Dynavax expects the U.S. market to grow from $615 million in 2024 to approximately $900 million by 2030 and that Heplisav-B’s market share will reach 60%.

This would translate to $540 million in U.S. net sales, and Sanofi only needs to plug the product into its sales infrastructure and achieve significant leverage from the transaction. This is already a good business as Dynavax managed to generate $35 million in EBITDA in the third quarter on $90 million in net sales.

It is also possible that Sanofi can achieve higher market penetration and it can also try and expand into other geographies other than Germany where Bavarian Nordic holds the rights.

With patent protection at least into 2032, I believe Heplisav-B alone is likely worth more than $2.2 billion Sanofi is paying for Dynavax – based on the current Street consensus, cumulative revenues between 2026 and 2032 should be approximately $4 billion, with the potential for a decent sized tail thereafter (assuming Sanofi can extend patent protection beyond 2032).

Upside from shingles vaccine and other pipeline projects

Dynavax’s pipeline consists of several other vaccine programs, and it seems Sanofi is most interested in the shingles (herpes zoster) vaccine program Z-1018.

Shingrix has set a high bar for efficacy – a 97% reduction in the incidence of shingles per 1,000 person-years, but its tolerability still leaves a lot to be desired.

Vaccination with Shingrix results in high rates of pain, redness, swelling, muscle pain, fatigue, and fever, and Dynavax has sought to take advantage of this by utilizing a CpG 1018 adjuvant in its shingles vaccine to try to improve tolerability “while maintaining comparable efficacy due to its ability to generate high levels of CD4+ cell responses, which is key in controlling reactivation of the zoster virus and preventing shingles.”

Preliminary phase 1/2 results look good with comparable immunogenicity to Shingrix.

And while the trial has a small sample of participants, the theoretical tolerability advantage seems to be holding up with much lower rates of local and systemic post-injection reactions compared to Shingrix.

The part 2 of the phase 1/2 trial will compare the tolerability, safety, and immunogenicity of Z-1018 and Shingrix in 324 subjects aged 70 and older, and should offer additional de-risking of this program.

This looks like a promising program, and the good news for Sanofi is that the value of the buyout leaves a lot of upside from the success of Z-1018.

The global shingles market size was estimated at approximately $4.2 billion in 2024 and it is dominated by GSK’s (GSK) Shingrix, which has generated £830m (approximately $1.1 billion) in net sales in the third quarter.

The total addressable market will likely grow over $5 billion by the time Z-1018 is approved (assuming it is successful in clinical trials), and similar efficacy to Shingrix and improved tolerability will likely result in strong market share gains in the 2030s.

The other programs were not mentioned in the press release, and I would want to see whether Sanofi plans to pursue any of them. These programs are also in an earlier stage of development, and there is no clinical data to assess their prospects.

Conclusion

The acquisition of Dynavax brings Heplisav-B, a hepatitis B vaccine with a decent revenue base and growth prospects, an early-stage shingles vaccine candidate Z-1018 and several additional vaccine programs.

I believe Heplisav-B alone is enough to justify the $2.2 billion price tag and that the success of Z-1018 would make this one of the better acquisitions Sanofi has made to date as the peak sales potential of a shingles vaccine is likely multiples of Heplisav-B.

The risks for Sanofi are Heplisav-B underperforming sales expectations and Z-1018 failing to show similar efficacy and/or better tolerability compared to Shingrix.

I have not covered Dynavax before and can only offer a neutral rating at this point due to the pending acquisition by Sanofi.

data.title: Omeros: Stock Pops On Yartemlea Approval, Longer-Term Upside Likely
data.description:
Investment Overview

Omeros (OMER) stock surged sharply today, after the company announced the FDA approval of its drug candidate narsoplimab in the indication of hematopoietic stem cell transplant-associated thrombotic microangiopathy (TA-TMA). The drug will be marketed and sold under the brand name Yartemlea.

Omeros stock is trading at $15.64 (at the time of writing), up nearly 80% for the day, and at its highest value since August 2021, while the Seattle based biotech's market cap valuation has crept to over $1bn.

It is the second time in only a few months that Omeros stock has surged in value - in my last note on the company, shared with Seeking Alpha readers in October, I covered the news that Omeros had agreed to an asset purchase and license agreement with Danish pharma giant Novo Nordisk (NVO) relating to its candidate zaltenibart.

Omeros is eligible to receive $240 million in upfront and near-term milestone payments, and up to a total of $2.1 billion including potential development and commercial milestones, plus tiered royalties on net sales.

In that note, I awarded the stock a Buy rating, which now seems to be justified by today's surge in the stock value. I noted that:

A biologics license application ("BLA") requesting FDA approval for narsoplimab from the Food and Drug Administration ("FDA") was rejected in 2021, but after discussions, in late 2024, Omeros seemed to suggest it had reached an agreement with the agency around data required to resubmit its application, sending shares soaring in value to over $12 per share.

In December, Omeros reported that narsoplimab had met its primary endpoint in a pivotal study in stem cell transplant patients and that it would resubmit its BLA as soon as possible. In May, the FDA accepted the BLA, assigning a decision date of September 25th, which was later postponed until December 26th.

Omeros had noted that:

The BLA resubmission includes the primary set of analyses comparing overall survival for narsoplimab-treated patients to overall survival for an external control group of TA-TMA patients. Results of those analyses showed clinically meaningful and statistically significant improvements in survival associated with narsoplimab treatment.

This seems to have done the trick, and seemingly, Omeros now has an exciting and potentially value accretive 2026 in store. Let's dig a little deeper into the commercial opportunity in play.

Analysis: Yartemlea's Commercial Potential

Here is how Omeros summarizes the factors that led to the FDA approving its drug candidate in a press release:

  • First and only approved option: YARTEMLEA® is the only approved treatment for hematopoietic stem cell transplant-associated thrombotic microangiopathy (TA-TMA) and is indicated for adults and children ages two years and older.
  • High complete response ("CR") rates: YARTEMLEA-treated patients achieved CR rates of 61% in the pivotal trial and 68% in the Expanded Access Program (EAP) among those with evaluable patient-level response data; CR defined as improvement in key laboratory values plus either improved organ function or transfusion independence.
  • Strong survival benefit: 100-day survival from TA-TMA diagnosis, based on all-cause mortality, was 73% in the pivotal trial and 74% in evaluable EAP patients; all patients met international harmonization criteria for high-risk TA-TMA.
  • Safety profile and Prescribing Information highlights: The only approved TA-TMA therapy, YARTEMLEA has no Boxed Warning and no Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (REMS), and vaccinations are not required prior to treatment. Serious infections and other adverse reactions, regardless of causality, have occurred in patients treated with YARTEMLEA; see Important Safety Information for details.

TA-TMA is a complication that can arise in as many as 39% of patients undergoing allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation ("HCT"), which has a less than 20% one-year survival rate, Omeros notes in a recent corporate presentation. The presentation also illustrates narsoplimab's mechanism of action ("MoA"), as shown below:

And below, Omeros outlines the commercial opportunity:

Analysts have speculated a list price of ~$150k for Yartemlea, so we may calculate that 39% of 30.5k patients multiplied by $150k translates to a market opportunity of ~$4.58bn, however, given pharma companies rarely receive the full list price, owing to discounts and rebates negotiated with patients and insurers, and given penetration will not be 100%, and given the drug is not approved in Europe, the peak revenue figure I arrive at is ~$300m (20% market penetration, average selling price $75k, 20k patient population).

Omeros says that "TA-TMA and narsoplimab ICD-10 codes in place create reimbursement hurdles for off-label treatments, however my gut feeling is that Yartemlea revenues may grow slowly, partly due to a lack of awareness of the drug (which can only be overcome with tens of millions of marketing spend, I'd speculate), and partly due to Omeros' limited funding - as of the end of Q3, the company reported a year-to-date net loss of $(89.8m), or $(1.39) per share, and cash plus short term investment of ~$36m. Current liabilities of $(81m) were also reported, along with $72m of convertible senior notes, and $87.4m of term debt.

I would in fact expect management to announce and complete a fundraising very soon, with shares trading at such a high value, so I'd caution that investors may experience some dilution in the short-term. My guess would be the company may look to raise as much as ~$300m. Interestingly, on its Q3 earnings call with analysts, management stated that:

With respect to revenues, we do not -- as you know, we just customarily don't talk about our revenue projections for a reasonable period of time until we're able to see the same revenue trend lines that everyone else is.

So I'm going to beg off of the revenue question other than to say that we believe that through the Novo transaction and the narsoplimab or YARTEMLEA approval that we would expect to potentially be cash flow positive in '27.

Looking Ahead - Is Omeros Stock A Buy, Sell Or Hold Headed Into 2026?

With Omeros stock now up 370% on a six-month basis, is there any further upside for stock to explore, or should investors bide their time, anticipating some stock dilution, and await the first commercial sales data before making any buy or sell decisions?

If we truly consider Yartemlea to be a ~$300m peak revenue asset, then today's valuation would feel about right (in my view) were it Omeros' only asset of note.

We know, however, that the company may also earn up to $2.1bn in milestones from Novo Nordisk (NVO) based on the success of zaltenibart.

As we can see, the MASP inhibitor franchise could secure several approvals in the coming years (if data supports it), including in paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH) - a rare, acquired blood disorder where the body's immune system mistakenly attacks and destroys red blood cells, leading to low levels of healthy red blood cells and other complications. I noted in my last post:

There are several drugs approved to treat PNH, including AstraZeneca's (AZN) Soliris and Ultomiris, both complement system inhibitors targeting the protein C5, that earned, respectively, $2.6bn and $3.9bn of revenues last year (Ultomiris is also approved to treat myasthenia gravis). Novartis' (NVS) Fabhalta (iptacopan) - $201m revenues in 1H25, and Apellis Pharmaceuticals' (APLS) Empaveli, ~$21m revenues last quarter.

As such, I wonder if zaltenibart, if not delivering the full $2.1bn of milestone payouts, could be a source of, say, ~$100m revenue annually from the time it is approved (assuming it is approved) for up to ~10 years.

The substance abuse franchise is intriguing, although I suspect it may be unwise to attach too much value to these programs until, or if, they progress into pivotal studies.

I also believe M&A could be a potential factor - would Novo consider acquiring the company to benefit from Yartemlea's success, and avoid paying so many milestones for zaltenibart? A bid of $2.5bn might do the trick, I'd speculate.

In my last note, I wrote:

After yesterday's gains, Omeros' market cap valuation is ~$700m, but I wonder if that valuation may have crept >$1bn come the end of 2025 if the FDA gives narsoplimab the thumbs up in December.

According to Omeros' website, current CEO Gregory A. Demopulos, MD, founded the company and has been at the helm since 1994, while David W Ghesquiere - formerly managing director of Adrenaline Venture & Advisory LLC, an international advisory firm, advising biotech and technology companies - joined as Chief Business Development Officer in August 2024. Nadia Dac has served as Chief Commercial Officer since January 2021, and was formerly at Alder Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (acquired in 2019 by Lundbeck in a ~$2bn deal).

In 2026, much may depend on the ability of the management team to work together to pivot Omeros into a successful commercial pharma, as opposed to biotech drug developer.

I think the striking of the Novo deal may illustrate management has what it takes to thrive in a commercial setting, so all things considered, I am going to update my thesis with another Buy rating, and posit that, come the end of 2026, Omeros' valuation will have has risen to over $1.5bn.

data.title: How The S&P 500 Reaches 10,000 In 2026
data.description:
Written by Sam Kovacs

Introduction

As I sit down to write this article, the S&P 500 is setting new record highs on Christmas Eve, surpassing 6,900 and getting awfully close to the 7,000 target I set half way through the year.

All I wanted for Christmas was some green candles, and it seems my wishes are coming true.

So now I find myself asking: Where to next? What route is the market set to take in 2026?

This time last year, I was saying that I predicted the S&P 500 (SPY) (SPX) would have a blow-off top at 6,700 to 6,900. Half way through the year, I updated my target to 7,000.

That prediction came in quite close, I should say. Sure, I also made some that were wildly off. For instance, I called for Bitcoin (BTC-USD) at $200-$300K, which ended up being a costly mistake for my crypto exposure.

But generally speaking, we got more right than wrong in 2025. So before you troll me for even considering S&P 500 at 10,000, here are some of the other predictions we made at the beginning of the year.

In a recent playbook we shared with members of the DFT, we called for:

  • A continuation of the AI trade and letting our Broadcom pick (AVGO) ride. AVGO is up 50% since.
  • Investment banks to do well as the AI cycle needed deals. Suggested positions in Goldman Sachs (GS), Morgan Stanley (MS), Deutsche Bank (DB), and Barclays (BCS) all outperformed the market significantly.
  • Positions in electrification plays like NextEra Energy (NEE) and HASI (HASI), which are up 10% and 18%, respectively, year over year.
  • Bearish oil and gas, with a hunch WTI was heading down. Crude oil dropped 17% this year.
  • I called for a moderation in inflation, and for the yield curve to disinvert and come down more, and for REITs to get their turn. This only partially happened and is still in play.

The point I'm trying to make is that while my calls are not perfect, I'm not an idiot either.

And when around me I see the likes of Ray Dalio say "We are 80% of a bubble", and Paul Tudor Jones saying that "now is potentially more explosive than 1999," I have to step back and say, "What if?"

What if this is a pre-2000 moment or a pre-1929 moment? What would that look like? S&P 500 to 10,000? After all, the market went up 40% in its last year before 2000 and 90% before 1929.

So there is a precedent. So it is possible. Now, is it probable? That's an entirely different question that requires we step back and invert.

Rather than asking, "will the S&P 500 go to 10,000?" we ask, "what needs to be true for the S&P 500 to go to 10,000?"

That's what this article is. It is the thought experiment of what needs to align, what needs to happen, for 2026 to be a go-go year of exuberance.

Being successful in markets comes down to understanding possible paths, giving some sense of probability to each, and then being willing to change your mind when the data tells you to.

Let's start our thought experiment.

Our starting point.

To project forward, let's already take stock of where we are today. The S&P 500 is already trading above long term valuation norms, with its TTM P/E ratio at 31x.

On a forward earnings basis, however, we're trading at 23x, which is much more in line with post 2015 norms, although still somewhat elevated.

So this gives us a first clue: Earnings growth is expected as the baseline. More on this later.

From an economy point of view, we have GDP, which has grown at the highest rate in years in Q3 at 4.3% YoY.

Inflation has come down and is expected to keep cooling as declining shelter inflation finally makes its way into headline numbers, something I've been calling for over a year as a measurement inefficiency that gives us a clear window into the future.

We're entering into a year where consumers will benefit from large tax refunds from the One Big Beautiful Bill and lower withholding taxes increasing take home pay, which should support consumers.

Finally, we're getting a potential reorganization of the Federal Reserve's leadership as Fed Chair Jerome Powell's term as chair ends in May, and President Donald Trump has said that a desire to cut rates aggressively will be the litmus test for the next chair.

So this is our starting point.

What does SPX 10,000 imply?

If we use Citi's 2026 EPS estimate of $320 for the S&P 500 as our baseline, 10,000 would imply 31x next year's earnings. Interestingly, on a TTM basis, that would be the same as it currently is.

So effectively, it would imply that not only is the high level of expected growth is realized, but that the market is expecting it to repeat in 2027.

From the level of 6,900 today (give or take a few points), a surge to 10,000 means a 44% rally from the current level.

Now this is possible, of course, although not common.

We have rallied about 40% from April's lows, for context.

In 1933 the market was up 54%, in 1954 it was up 52%, in 1928 it was up 43.6%.

The top 10 years of the S&P 500's history over the past 100 years have all delivered returns of more than 34%.

So this is a massive PE rerating story, but one that has historical precedent.

For this to happen, fuel needs to be added to the fire. Like my favorite George Soros quote says:

When I see a bubble forming, I rush in to buy, adding fuel to the fire.

For this to continue unabated, we need to see a good story happen on four fronts: Rates, inflation, flows, and earnings.

Why rates will likely come down more

The Fed's dot plot currently suggests just one more rate cut between now and the end of the year 2026.

While this is cute, it's mostly just laziness and unwillingness to properly consider the political shifts that are happening.

Let's be clear, there are two reasons that Trump and Scott Bessent want rates significantly lower next year:

  • The first is political, next year is an election year. Don't need to say much more here.
  • The second is just fiscal math. Interest costs are projected to be around $1 trillion in 2026 and rise from there if yields are not subdued.

Now, of course, the executive branch doesn't control rates, and global markets demand a perception of Fed independence, but the fiscal imperative and timing of new appointments mean that political narratives and new appointments can make for a change in the discourse at the Fed.

And, of course, inflation needs to play ball, but if it does the likelihood of further cuts is probably greater than what is currently being priced by markets.

Inflation to stay subdued.

For the valuation melt-up to hold, we need a solid re-anchoring of inflation expectations to the Fed's long term target of 2%.

Shelter inflation is continuing to cool. Large firms are implementing AI to accelerate output per worker, with many already reporting productivity gains. This would be a disinflationary pressure. Let's see if it comes into play. Then if we add in no major commodity shock, no sustained shipping or supply chain shock, then inflation should come back down.

To be truly frank, shelter alone should probably make the war on inflation practically over. Shelter is 35% of the CPI basket, and it's the largest lagging domino in the CPI story.

Last year rent and OER were still running at 4.5-4.9% year over year. Now these are down to 3%.

In reality, market rents have declined year over year by about 1%, according to ApartmentList and Zillow estimates.

There are a few reasons. Headlines would like to tell you it's because migrants got sent home, but the big reason is that a lot of apartments got built this year. Multifamily completions reached 608,000 units this year, which was the highest level since 1986.

So in this scenario, shelter should continue its drift down, which will continue to subdue the headlines.

This will empower markets to rerate yields lower, which will bring us to the third point: Flows.

Flows out of fixed income, into risk assets

There is about $7.6 trillion in money market funds today.

The yields on these drop quite quickly as they are short duration, and investors that were happy earning risk-free yields now ask themselves what to do with this cash.

Of course, not all of the $7 trillion finds its way into equities, but a portion certainly does, especially if the market catches a bid above 7,000 and people start to fear they are missing out on the market.

Second, as yields and rates come down, bond prices go up. Here institutional portfolios might rebalance quarterly to stay within set limits and reallocate part of their bond gains and rebalance risk into equities.

What's more, in such a scenario, we'd start to hear echoes of a soft landing achieved, plus rate cut expectations being changed, credit spreads would likely compress, which would force assets out the risk curve, piling into equity, crypto, and the like.

Good conditions create earnings reflexivity and psychological knock on effects.

Lower interest rates increase the profitability of corporates, which frees up cash that gets plowed into share buybacks, and an increased M&A appetite brings big deals that add to valuations.

EPS numbers go up and exceed the already aggressive projections.

The narrative about AI and how it is changing the world leads to more "this time is different" narratives, which become socially acceptable. A dovish Fed, the fiscal argument for lower rates, and the calm inflation get everyone excited.

The supportive fiscal and monetary policy gives people a sense that not only the Fed Put is back, but that there's a Trump put or a government put in play that will not leave markets go down.

People feel protected, and across the board we see risk premia adjust down.

Some will certainly be skeptical, but they're forced to benchmark against peers who are making money, and their career risk forces them to continue playing and even triggers some late-cycle FOMO from institutions.

Finally, we see volatility fall, which gives people a sense of safety, and pushes more leverage into the markets across the board.

Of course, this is the mania part of the bubble, but it's what's required to give such an aggressive push in 2026.

Conclusion

Of course, all bubbles need to be pricked. And this one would be no exception.

After unsustainable demand of securities leads to unsustainable valuations, something in the narrative switches, which causes us to reverse course.

It might be a reignition of inflation on the back of "tariff dividend checks," it might be a change in the rate of earnings growth under expectations, it could be anything really!

What's important to note, though, is that to get to 10,000, while we need euphoria, that euphoria needs to be the product of a market that comes to believe that the rules of the game have changed, that policymakers are competent and will not stand in the way, and that risks, which in the past might have put a cap on valuations, are no longer valid.

For that to be the case, a certain set of conditions needs to exist.

Now, is this my base case for 2026? No.

Is it put away in the possible but not probable? Neither.

I'd say the lining up of the stars that would cause such an environment at probably a one in eight chance.

Not something to count on, but not something to discount.

Happy holidays to those of you celebrating, and wish that this article has given you something to think about.

data.title: Ramaco Resources: Buyback Strength, But The Cycle Still Matters
data.description:
The announcement of the $100MM buyback plan in the last few days made Ramaco Resources, Inc. (METC) stock rise sharply, but beyond this move, the question that I ask at this moment is another: is the company really in a position to return capital at this point in the cycle?

After reviewing the Q3 2025 numbers and the earnings call, my answer is yes, but with nuances. Although the quarter was weak in results, it showed us something that today is worth more than growth, that the problem is not execution but the context of the cycle. Despite lower prices and volumes, METC was able to maintain positive EBITDA, control costs and a good cash position.

In this context, my stance is Hold, as I believe that the post-announcement rally has already captured much of the buy-back impact, and I also note that the cycle context remains challenging. While I see no clear short-term catalysts, there is also no reason to leave as long as the company continues to demonstrate this ability to withstand the cycle.

Q3 2025: weak numbers, but a clear strategy

In Q3 2025, Ramaco had revenues of $121 MM, a decline of 27.7% YoY, and a net loss that translated into a negative EPS of $0.25. They are not good numbers, and management did not try to hide them.

However, I think it is important to mention that in the context where coal prices continued to fall, with indices in the U.S. almost 20% below the previous year, Ramaco decided not to sell tons at a loss just to sustain volume, as highlighted by the CEO during the call:

We made what seems like a very logical decision to refuse to sell tons at a loss into an oversaturated market. We are fortunate that we now have the strongest liquidity position we have ever had, which allows us the flexibility to take this posture.

This decision is reflected in production and costs. In Q3, METC produced 945,000 tons and sold nearly 900,000, below Q2. In return, it managed to maintain cash cost at around $97/ton, ranking among the lowest-cost producers in the U.S. There was even an indication that in September costs fell further, to levels close to $86 per ton.

Adjusted EBITDA was $8.4 MM, just below Q2, and net loss was $13 MM, very similar to the previous quarter. For me, this shows that, even with adverse prices, the business does not fall apart.

Record liquidity: the basis for repurchase

The repurchase cannot be analyzed without looking at the balance sheet. At Q3 close, Ramaco reported total liquidity of $272 MM, the highest level in its history, and a net cash position of $77 MM.

During the quarter, the company raised $200 MM in equity, refinanced debt and extended maturities. The result is simple, and today it translates into financial flexibility, something unusual for a met coal producer in this part of the cycle.

That’s why when I read about the buyback plan for up to $100 MM in 24 months it didn’t sound aggressive to me. The CEO himself made it clear:

The share repurchase program highlights our confidence in our continued operational performance as well as financial strength.

For me, the buy-back works more as a signal than an immediate catalyst of EPS. It marks a psychological floor and shows that the management believes that the current price does not reflect the long-term value, even recognizing the bad moment of the market.

Met coal today, rare earths and critical minerals to future

In the short term, METC remains a producer of metallurgical coal, heavily dependent on the steel cycle and the pressure generated by Chinese exports. The management itself was clear when talking about 2026 and did not mention a rapid price recovery.

At the same time, the call made it clear that Brook Mine and the critical minerals business are METC’s long-term bet:

We just announced that we intend to establish a national strategic stockpile and terminal for rare earths and critical minerals at our Brook Mine. We’re calling it the Strategic Critical Minerals Terminal.

There was talk of the pilot plant, of the possibility of scaling up the project and of significant economic potential, but today this still does not translate into results or cash. That is why I do not take it as a central part of the current valuation.

I see it more as a future bonus, something that can add value over time if the execution goes well. And that "extra" starts to be more relevant when the main business is in good shape, with enough liquidity and costs under control to get through the cycle without a hitch.

How far can the stock go?

With the stock close to $18 after the rally, I don’t see a very big immediate upside without a clear improvement in met coal prices, since an important part of the buy-back is already in the price.

In a base scenario, with stable prices and no further deterioration, I see Ramaco moving in a reasonable range of $16 to $20, with the repurchase acting as support, while, to move to a more positive scenario, I believe there is a need for concrete signs of market recovery or tangible progress on contracts and execution at Brook Mine.

Risks that cannot be ignored

The main risk remains the price of metallurgical coal, since if the cycle lasts longer than expected, results will remain under pressure, even with good operational execution.

I also see risks in critical mineral projects. Brook Mine is certainly attractive in the long run, but we know it takes time, investment and good execution, and none of that is guaranteed today.

Finally, the stock is volatile and with little liquidity, something quite common in companies of this size and sector, and which can generate sudden movements that do not always reflect real changes in the business.

Conclusion

After the recent news and the movement associated with it, my stance is Hold. I do not see METC as a Buy at these levels, but as a company that improved its risk profile thanks to record liquidity, operational discipline and a clear signal of return to the shareholder.

The $100 MM buyback does not change the cycle of metallurgical coal, which remains the main factor to be monitored. What it does show us is that Ramaco comes to this moment with more room for manoeuvre than many of its peers. In a sector where several companies are focused on survival, METC can afford to return capital without compromising the balance sheet, and that for me is a plus point, even though the context remains challenging.

data.title: Esquire Financial Holdings: Shares Are Becoming Difficult To Justify
data.description:
In the past, I had been rather bullish about Esquire Financial Holdings, Inc. (ESQ). Back in June 2024, I rated the company a "Strong Buy." And from that point until I downgraded the company all the way to a "Hold" in September of this year, the stock had performed spectacularly, skyrocketing 114.1% while the S&P 500 was up only 19.8%. But such a massive amount of upside, especially for a bank, justified in my view a recalibration of expectations. Although the institution is growing nicely, valuation became problematic. Yes, asset quality was high enough to justify some sort of premium. At the end of the day, however, I felt that the premium in question became too high.

My timing was perhaps not ideal. Since downgrading the company in September, the stock has risen another 9.7%. That comfortably outpaces the 4.6% rise of the S&P 500 over the same window of time. But I doubt that my previous decision was wrong. In fact, I feel more confident now than in the past. Yes, the institution continues to grow nicely. And yes, that trend is likely to continue. On top of this, asset quality remains robust. At the end of the day, however, shares can only rise so much. And valuation will become a bit of a problem if this trend continues.

Shares Are Too Expensive For My Liking

The only new data that we currently have regarding Esquire Financial Holdings covers through the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, deposits for the institution came in at $1.88 billion. This is up nicely from the $1.78 billion the company had in the second quarter, and it represents a big jump over the $1.64 billion the institution had at the end of last year. To some extent, this should not be surprising. This is, as I've detailed in previous articles, a very niche bank. The overwhelming majority of its deposits are focused on litigation-related activities in the legal sector. In fact, litigation and payment processing deposits accounted for a full 76% and 8%, respectively, of overall deposits. The company has essentially turned itself into the go-to shop for the litigation industry. And in such a litigious country like what we live in today, it's not difficult to imagine that this market does have great growth potential.

In fact, management estimates that the litigation vertical is worth about $529 billion, with over 100,000 firms operating here. And naturally, the company is focused on other opportunities as well, including the $11.7 trillion payment processing vertical that has over 10 million merchants globally in it. My only complaint here is that uninsured deposit exposure is a bit higher than I would like it to be. I typically want to see no more than 30% of deposits be classified as uninsured. But according to management, total exposure now stands at 32%.

At the end of 2024, loans amounted to $1.38 billion. This exposure has now increased nicely to $1.53 billion. This is to be expected given the amount that deposits have increased by. And not surprisingly, a lot of this exposure also involves the litigation space. In fact, 64% of its loan portfolio falls under this category. Distant second place belongs to the multifamily asset category at 24%. But then we also have commercial real estate, one- to four-family real estate, consumer loans, and other commercial loans comprising the rest.

The company has also historically had a good chunk of change tied up in securities. The company ended the most recent quarter with $330.6 million worth. That's up slightly over the $313.4 million it had at the end of last year. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, went from $126.3 million all the way up to $240.8 million. And the company accomplished this at a time when it kept debt at nothing. Yes, this is one of the rare banks that has no debt on its books. I don't know the exact number. But from my own experiences, I would say that only a couple of banks that are publicly traded have this distinction. And that alone certainly justifies some premium.

As the balance sheet has increased, the income statement has gotten larger as well. Net interest income grew from $24.9 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $29.6 million in the third quarter of 2025. Unfortunately, not everything was great here. Even though the change in the size of the balance sheet helped, the company saw a reduction in net interest margin from 6.16% to 6.06%. On top of this, it saw an increase in the provision for credit losses from $1 million to $1.8 million.

Non-interest income has also been growing, but only very modestly. Driven primarily by higher customer service fees, service charges, and other miscellaneous sources of revenue, this metric grew from $6.1 million last year to $6.2 million this year. But for the first nine months of this year, the $19 million reported by management beat out the $18.7 million reported a year earlier. This, as well as the continued growth in net interest income, has proven instrumental in pushing net profits up from $11.4 million in the third quarter of last year to $14.1 million at the same time this year. And for the first nine months of this year, the $37.4 million reported by management exceeded the $31.9 million reported a year ago.

Assuming that the rest of this year looks similar to what we have seen already for 2025, we would have a price-to-earnings multiple for this bank of 16.4. This is significantly greater than the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. And as the chart above illustrates, it would put Esquire Financial Holdings above four of the five banks that I compared it to in it. But that's not the only way in which the institution is expensive. In the chart below, you can see its price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. In addition to being more than triple what I would typically like to see, it's also meaningfully higher than any of the five banks that I compared it to.

To some extent, a premium can be justified by high asset quality. And off the top of my head, I don't know if any bank that I've looked at has asset quality that matches or exceeds what Esquire Financial Holdings boasts. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 2.61%. That's well above the 1% minimum that I like to see. And it puts the company massively higher than any of these other five institutions I stacked it up against. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see the same thing regarding return on equity. The 20.83% reading that we get here is more than double the 10% minimum that I like to see. And it's also nearly double the next highest bank that I compared it to.

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, Esquire Financial Holdings continues to prove that it is a high-quality institution. The company is growing nicely, and asset quality is top-notch. But unfortunately, that comes with some downsides. Specifically, shares are expensive right now. If it weren't for how high quality the assets of the institution were, I would actually be bearish about this business. But with that factored in, I think that maintaining it as a "Hold" candidate is the right move. However, if we see the share price continue to rise like it has, a further downgrade could be in order.

data.title: ZIM Integrated Shipping: Nice Gains, Weaker Outlook
data.description:
Article Thesis

ZIM Integrated Shipping Services Ltd. (ZIM) has recently shown a strong performance thanks to expectations about the company getting acquired. While I recommended buying ZIM in the past, I'm somewhat less bullish now -- the dividend yield has declined, and depending on how the M&A process goes, there's some potential for unhappiness.

Past Coverage

I have written about ZIM here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in May 2025, when I wrote this article. I recommended buying ZIM back then on the back of a high dividend yield and a good operational performance. This worked out well so far, with ZIM returning 18% since that article was published, which was slightly more than the broad market's performance over the same time frame.

What Happened?

This week, ZIM Integrated Shipping experienced nice gains, as the market reacted positively to the company's update regarding its strategic review process.

Before that update was released, we had already gotten the news that there had been some bidding for ZIM Integrated Shipping, with Hapag-Lloyd (HPGLY, HLAGF) being among the frontrunners, although MSC Shipping stated they aren't interested in acquiring ZIM Integrated Shipping, even though there had been rumors about an MSC bid for ZIM.

ZIM Integrated Shipping is a shipping company with a pretty solid balance sheet and, depending on market conditions, quite good profitability and cash flow levels at times. I thus think that it is not too surprising that there are parties interested in acquiring ZIM Integrated Shipping, and since ZIM also doesn't have an overly high market capitalization, at around $2.5 billion, it should be an easy-to-digest target for a company like Hapag-Lloyd, which is valued at around $24 billion, almost ten times the market capitalization of ZIM -- financing such a deal should thus be relatively easy for Hapag-Lloyd or other large-sized potential acquirers.

ZIM Integrated Shipping's strategic review update states that there are several interested parties that have made bids for ZIM, although ZIM doesn't offer a list of names for now. An exception to that is the revised/second offer made by ZIM's own CEO and an entity he owns -- CEO Eli Glickman and related parties have made an offer for ZIM that was, however, not attractive enough, according to ZIM's board of directors, which is why it was declined.

While the strategic review isn't over, and while we do not have a lot of hard facts about what it will ultimately mean for the company, it's positive that there are different parties that are interested in buying ZIM. This suggests that the company is attractive and not overly expensive at current prices. It is also positive that the company's board of directors doesn't eagerly jump on the first bid it received, but that they are instead focused on getting the highest-possible value for shareholders. The fact that ZIM's own CEO has made a second bid that -- at least according to the company's board -- undervalues the company is a bit of a concern, however: There's some risk that this insider is acting in his own interest instead of trying to create the most value for ZIM's shareholders.

ZIM Integrated Shipping's strategic review may result in the sale of the entire company eventually, but it's also possible that ZIM may decide to sell parts of the company, make changes to its dividend strategy, etc. Shareholders seem positive for now, with ZIM trading up by a little more than 20% over the last month. There is some risk that the strategic review ultimately doesn't result in a lot of shareholder value creation, though -- maybe ZIM just declines all offers and decides to keep everything as it is. If that were to happen, ZIM's shares could pull back, I believe, as the current enthusiasm would likely wane.

ZIM Integrated Shipping: Operating Performance

The shipping industry is famously volatile, and that especially holds true for container liners such as ZIM Integrated Shipping without a significant portion of revenues being locked in for the long term. Ups and downs in ZIM's results are thus to be expected, and we saw that again when ZIM reported its Q3 results.

The company generated revenues of around $1.8 billion during the quarter, which was down by more than 35% compared to one year earlier. This was caused by slightly lower transportation volumes and a severe pricing decline -- ZIM's average freight rate during the most recent quarter was down by more than one-third compared to one year earlier.

Since costs for ZIM are largely fixed, revenue declines usually result in big margin hits. Of course, the reversal is true during times when ZIM's revenues expand -- then margins can soar. The Q3 decline in revenues made ZIM's net profits decline by more than 80%, to $123 million. This is still a pretty strong level of profitability, considering the company is valued at around $2.5 billion while the annualized net profit is roughly $500 million, but the trend clearly isn't our friend.

Management's guidance for the current year sees adjusted EBIT coming in at around $800 million, using the midpoint of the guidance range. Since ZIM already generated adjusted EBIT of $870 million in Q1 to Q3, the current guidance midpoint implies that Q4 will be a pretty weak quarter, underlining my belief that the trend is unfavorable for now. Of course, things could turn at some point in 2026, e.g. if the global economy picks up speed or trade tensions ease and more goods are moved around the world. But in the very near term, ZIM's results will likely not be very compelling -- the recent positive price movements thus seem driven by M&A expectations primarily, not by strong underlying business momentum.

ZIM's Dividend

ZIM Integrated Shipping has offered strong dividend payments in recent years, although with huge ups and downs in between. Earlier this year, ZIM's dividend yield stood at a pretty strong level of 15%, but between share price appreciation since then and lower dividend payments, ZIM's dividend yield has declined to around 6% for now, based on the most recent dividend announcement at $0.31 per share.

At least for income investors, ZIM Integrated Shipping has thus become less appealing, although those who have been buying ZIM in recent weeks likely don't care too much about the dividend and focus on potential upside if ZIM receives a big bid.

Is ZIM Integrated Shipping An Attractive Investment?

I have been bullish on ZIM at times in the past, but I'm not overly bullish for now. The market has worsened for container liners, with ZIM seeing lower volumes, lower prices, and much lower profits. Dividends have been reduced, and at the same time, ZIM's stock has become pricier.

While ZIM Integrated Shipping may work out well as a speculative investment in case the company gets a big bid from Hapag-Lloyd, Maersk, or another shipping giant, this is a somewhat risky bet, I think. If it turns out that no high-enough bid will be made, enthusiasm could wane, and ZIM's shares could pull back, especially if the Q4 report turns out to be bad. At least for now, I'm thus not bullish on ZIM Integrated Shipping any longer, although an investment in ZIM may still make sense for those with a different investment approach focused on special situations, M&A potential, and so on.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
